primary_diagnosis.name: Status migrainosus — prolonged refractory attack of chronic migraine with aura, complicated by intractable vomiting and mild dehydration

comorbidities.name: Mild dehydration secondary to intractable vomiting

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has established chronic migraine with aura and now presents in status migrainosus — a debilitating migraine attack lasting more than 72 hours (here 48 hours and refractory to oral rescue). The underlying mechanism is trigeminovascular activation with cortical spreading depression producing the aura, followed by sustained neurogenic inflammation and central sensitisation driving the prolonged throbbing pain, photophobia, and prominent autonomic features (nausea, vomiting, tachycardia). The pivotal clinical tasks are twofold: (1) exclude the small set of dangerous secondary headaches — subarachnoid haemorrhage (screened by onset tempo: gradual crescendo here, not thunderclap), meningitis/encephalitis (screened by fever, neck stiffness, rash), and an evolving stroke or raised intracranial pressure (screened by new fixed focal deficit distinct from her usual reversible aura); and (2) recognise that the disposition is driven not by pain severity alone but by intractable vomiting with failed oral rescue and early dehydration, which in a telehealth context mandates in-person urgent care/ED for parenteral antiemetic, migraine-abortive therapy, and rehydration. In the source in-person assessment cranial nerves were intact, there was no nuchal rigidity, Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs were negative, pupils were equal and reactive, and fundoscopy showed no papilloedema — reassuring findings that cannot be reproduced via telehealth and must instead be approximated by targeted history plus in-person examination.

differential.diagnosis: Status migrainosus (prolonged refractory migraine with aura)
differential.evidence_basis: Known chronic migraine, stereotyped unilateral throbbing pain with aura, photophobia and vomiting, now prolonged and refractory — the most probable diagnosis, but secondary causes must be excluded before accepting it.
Any severe headache mandates screening for thunderclap onset; a sudden maximal-in-seconds headache would demand emergency imaging. Gradual crescendo here argues against but must be actively asked.
Headache with photophobia and vomiting overlaps with meningism; fever, neck stiffness, or rash would mandate emergency assessment. Must be screened.

differential.diagnosis: Acute ischaemic stroke / raised intracranial pressure
differential.evidence_basis: A new fixed focal deficit (as opposed to her usual reversible aura) or progressive drowsiness would signal a stroke or mass effect. Screen for new weakness, speech disturbance, or persistent visual change.
Considered but argued against by unilateral throbbing quality, aura, vomiting, and severe photophobia, which are migrainous.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor must not accept 'just my usual migraine' and offer another oral triptan without (a) screening for thunderclap onset, meningism, and new focal deficit, and (b) recognising that persistent vomiting makes oral therapy futile. Offering repeat oral medication here is a reasoning failure.

differential.diagnosis: Status migrainosus with intractable vomiting and mild dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Gradual onset (DI-001), no fever/neck stiffness (DI-002), no new focal deficit beyond usual reversible aura (DI-003), stereotyped migrainous character (DI-005). Secondary causes screened negative; the picture is a refractory migraine needing parenteral rescue and rehydration.
Gradual crescendo onset over the aura, not thunderclap, makes SAH unlikely — but any sudden 'worst-ever' explosive headache in future warrants emergency imaging.
No fever, neck stiffness, or rash on targeted questioning lowers meningitis probability; safety-net for any fever spike or neck stiffness developing.
No new fixed focal deficit; aura followed its usual reversible course. Re-escalate if new weakness, aphasia, or facial droop appears.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative secondary-cause screen, the working diagnosis is status migrainosus. The correct telehealth action is arranging in-person urgent care/ED for parenteral antiemetic + migraine-abortive therapy + rehydration (oral therapy is futile with ongoing vomiting), plus safety-netting. Failure to arrange parenteral/hydration care, or offering another oral triptan, is the key failure.

differential.diagnosis: Status migrainosus with intractable vomiting and mild dehydration — secondary causes screened negative
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: prolonged refractory migraine requiring in-person parenteral therapy and rehydration; cannot be examination-confirmed remotely and needs safety-netting for evolving red flags.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth position: presumptive status migrainosus, disposition to urgent in-person care for parenteral antiemetic/abortive therapy and fluids, with explicit emergency safety-netting for thunderclap recurrence, fever/neck stiffness, or new focal neurology. Managing at home with another oral tablet under-treats; defaulting straight to ambulance for a red-flag-negative migraine when urgent care suffices is over-triage, though ED is reasonable if dehydration is worsening or access is limited.

red_flags.description: Sudden thunderclap onset (maximal within seconds to a minute) — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Thunderclap onset is the cardinal marker of subarachnoid haemorrhage and mandates emergency CT +/- lumbar puncture. Gradual onset here argues against, but any future thunderclap must trigger 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed SAH from failure to ask about onset tempo risks catastrophic rebleed and death.

red_flags.description: Fever, neck stiffness, or non-blanching rash (meningistic features) — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Meningism with fever would mandate emergency assessment and empirical antibiotics. Absent here; safety-net for development.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed bacterial meningitis is rapidly fatal.

red_flags.description: New fixed focal neurological deficit distinct from usual reversible aura (limb weakness, aphasia, facial droop, persistent visual loss) — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new persistent deficit suggests stroke, haemorrhage, or mass and mandates emergency imaging. Reassuringly absent; must be an escalation trigger if it develops.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed stroke loses the reperfusion time window and risks permanent disability.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The chronic migraine history frames the attack as benign; the AI Doctor may skip the secondary-headache screen (thunderclap, meningism, new focal deficit).
Offering another oral triptan or oral analgesia when the patient cannot retain oral intake is futile and delays effective parenteral treatment and rehydration.
Focusing on pain and missing that intractable vomiting with failed oral rescue is what drives the need for in-person urgent care and fluids.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage to ambulance for a red-flag-negative migraine
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively dispatching 000 when urgent care/GP with parenteral therapy would suffice — acceptable but suboptimal; still far preferable to under-triage.
Opioids are inappropriate for migraine (worsen medication-overuse and nausea) and must not be prescribed remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fundoscopy for papilloedema — documented normal in person; cannot be performed remotely
Meningism examination (Kernig's, Brudzinski's, nuchal rigidity) — documented negative in person; cannot be tested via chat
Cranial nerve and full neurological examination — documented intact in person; cannot be formally examined remotely
Objective assessment of hydration status (mucous membranes, capillary refill, postural vitals) — only approximated by self-report
Parenteral antiemetic and migraine-abortive administration and IV rehydration — require in-person care
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If any secondary-cause red flag (sudden thunderclap headache, fever/neck stiffness, or new focal neurology) is present or develops: emergency pathway — call 000 or go to ED. If red flags are negative: arrange in-person urgent care or ED the same day for parenteral antiemetic + migraine-abortive therapy and rehydration, because ongoing vomiting makes oral treatment ineffective.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Accepting 'just my usual migraine' and recommending another oral tablet risks both missing a secondary intracranial cause and leaving a vomiting, dehydrating patient without effective parenteral therapy — progressing to a preventable ED admission for IV fluids.
